chunks.text: v PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 0 Introduction 0.1 Background Health and safety statistics show that approximately 50 people are killed each year and more than 5 000 injured in accidents involving workplace transport [1]. Each workplace is unique and likely to present different hazards and risks. However, a well-designed and maintained workplace with proper segregation of vehicles and people will experience fewer workplace accidents [2]. The most effective way of ensuring pedestrians and vehicles move safely around a workplace is to provide separate pedestrian and vehicle traffic routes and, where necessary, implement barriers, rails and signage to prevent pedestrians crossing at dangerous places and to direct them to safer crossing points [2]. There is a lot of information from safety organizations about workplace transport. The Health and Safety Executive (HSE) states that workplace transport means any vehicle or piece of mobile equipment used in any work setting [3]. Segregation of pedestrians and vehicles is advised but the way to achieve this can only be determined by assessing all of the site-specific issues and risks. Warehousing and storage, a guide to health and safety (HSE) states [3]: “ Warehouses should be designed and laid out to allow for the safe movement of goods, materials and people. Good design and layout can help reduce accidents, including those involving vehicles and people slipping and tripping .” The movement of goods and materials involves the use of a wide range of vehicles and accounts for a large proportion of accidents in the workplace. It is important to have a safe system of traffic management, including methods and procedures for arrival, reception, unloading, loading and movement of vehicles within the workplace. People and vehicles should be segregated as far as is “reasonably practicable”. 1) 1)   Health and Safety Executive, ALARP “at a glance”, 2014, www.hse.gov.uk/risk/theory/alarpglance.htm. Mixing vehic
chunks.label: PAS 13 §0.1 — Background (p.7)

chunks.text: People and vehicles should be segregated as far as is “reasonably practicable”. 1) 1)   Health and Safety Executive, ALARP “at a glance”, 2014, www.hse.gov.uk/risk/theory/alarpglance.htm. Mixing vehicles and pedestrians increases the risk of potential accidents, so it is imperative that steps are taken to minimize the associated risks by applying the following: •   Separate pedestrian activities from areas where vehicles are operating, where reasonably practical. •   Define, designate and clearly mark pedestrian routes and crossing places. •   Use protective barriers and traffic management procedures to control the movement of vehicles and pedestrians. PAS 13 outlines the current good practice traffic management procedures for a workplace and provides a standard for the safety barriers within them. Frequently asked questions after determining the requirement for pedestrian and vehicle segregation include: •   What type of protection or safety barrier are to be used? •   When is a vehicle barrier, pedestrian barrier or white line used? •   Is the current protection or safety barrier fit for purpose? PAS 13 provides recommendations for these questions and give further understanding on how the selected protection may be rated and meet a specification and a performance rating by use of a test method. The test method element of the PAS describes dynamic impact tests and barrier performance measurement.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §0.1 — Background (p.7)

chunks.text: vi PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 0.2 Main clauses This PAS is divided into four main clauses that describe the testing and implementation of safety barriers, and best traffic management practices. 1)   Clause 4: Safety barriers used in traffic management within the workplace •   How safety barriers can be used to assist in the safe management of workplace traffic. 2)   Clause 5: Safety barrier design •   Principles of safety barrier design for individual applications and environments. 3)   Clause 6: Kinetic energy of vehicles •   How to calculate the impact kinetic energy of vehicles in a workplace. Once this potential energy is determined, safety barriers that withstand the impacts of specific vehicles can be selected. 4)   Clause 7: Methods of testing the force of impact that a safety barrier can withstand •   How to test, measure and rate a safety barrier for use within a workplace environment, with clear pass or fail criteria.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §0.2 — Main clauses (p.8)

chunks.text: 1 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 1 Scope This PAS gives recommendations for the impact resilience, dimensions and positions of safety barriers in the workplace, and guidance on how to manage the risks associated with vehicles within the workplace, and specifies the criteria for testing the impact resilience of a barrier. It is applicable to safety barriers used where there is a risk of collision between workplace vehicles or machinery and pedestrians. It is intended to be used by those who are concerned with selecting the appropriate safety barrier for their workplace to protect personnel, vehicles and structures, those who want to apply traffic management procedures and those who test and measure the performance of safety barriers. It may also be of interest to manufacturers and distributors of safety barriers. This PAS does not cover: •   highways and public environments; •   an installation specification or installation guide for safety barriers; •   safety barriers when working at height regulations. Traffic and pedestrian routes do not cover stairs, staircases and fixed ladders.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §1 — Scope (p.9)

chunks.text: 6 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 4 Safety barriers used in traffic management within the workplace 4.1 General NOTE   Traffic management involves the safe movement of vehicles and pedestrians through and around the workplace. This clause provides recommendations on when and where to use safety barriers to effectively manage health and safety risks associated with workplace traffic. 4.2 Assessing risks using a work area plan A floor plan of the workplace should be drafted to include the following: a)   pedestrian routes and walkways; b)   pedestrian zones and work areas; c)   vehicle routes; d)   pedestrian crossing points; e)   critical structures and equipment; f)   vehicle parking areas; and g)   loading and unloading zones. NOTE   An example is given in Figure 1. Figure 1   – Example floor plan of a workplace Key: 1.   Pedestrian routes and walkways 2.   Pedestrian zones and work areas 3.   Vehicle routes 4.   Pedestrian crossing points 5.   Critical structures and equipment 6.   Vehicle parking areas 7.   Loading and unloading zones
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.1 — General (p.14)

chunks.text: 7 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 NOTE   The floor plan allows evaluation of each element for risks, and to assess: a)   the severity of the risks; b)   whether existing control measures are effective; c)   the action to take to control the risks; and d)   the urgency required to control the risks. 4.3 Control measures Control measures should be ranked and implemented using the following hierarchical list: a)   eliminate the hazard; NOTE   It is most efficient to eliminate hazards when a workplace is first planned out. b)   substitute with something safer; NOTE   For example automated equipment. c)   isolate the hazard from pedestrians; NOTE   For example segregate vehicles and pedestrians. d)   improve the workplace design and layout around vehicles and critical structures; e)   use engineering controls such as speed limiters on vehicles; f)   install administrative controls; NOTE   For example, signs and marked areas. g)   use personal protective equipment (PPE); NOTE   For example, high-visibility clothing. h)   information/instruction/training for both pedestrians and plant operators. 4.4 Reviewing control measures Controls should be continually reviewed to determine whether: a)   the control measures are effective; b)   there been any changes to the environment; c)   the controls have created any new problems; d)   the procedures have being followed correctly; e)   there have been improvements to health and safety; and NOTE   For example, accidents and near misses have been reduced. f)   there are lessons to be learnt from accident, incident and near miss reports and have these been applied. 4.5 Pedestrian routes, walkways and work area zones 4.5.1 Assessing and evaluating The current pedestrian traffic routes and walkways should be assessed and evaluated as follows: a)   Do the pedestrian routes correspond to the desire lines? b)   Can the pedestrian routes be placed where no vehicle traffic occurs? c)   Are walkways wide enough for pedes
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.5.1 — Assessing and evaluating (p.15)

chunks.text: nd evaluated as follows: a)   Do the pedestrian routes correspond to the desire lines? b)   Can the pedestrian routes be placed where no vehicle traffic occurs? c)   Are walkways wide enough for pedestrian and wheelchair accessibility? NOTE 1   See   5.9   and Table A.1 when assessing the minimum width of a pedestrian walkway that is leading to an emergency exit. NOTE 2   Walkway widths may be subject to regional variations in building regulations.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.5.1 — Assessing and evaluating (p.15)

chunks.text: 8 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 4.5.2 Control measures for pedestrian routes, walkways and work area zones 4.5.2.1   Where vehicles and pedestrians cannot be segregated, pedestrian routes should have the following control measures, as shown in Figure 2: a)   a walkway or pedestrian zone of a minimum width of 600 mm; b)   wheelchair access of a minimum width of 1 200 mm; c)   when a safety barrier is used, the maximum deflection of the barrier is added to the width of the pedestrian zone; d)   pedestrian areas are entirely free from vehicles; e)   workplaces are designed so there is no need for a vehicle to enter a pedestrian zone; f)   clear markings and signs are used to set vehicle and pedestrian areas apart; g)   raised kerbs are used to better define the traffic and pedestrian routes; h)   coloured walkways are used to better define pedestrian routes; i)   an opportunity is provided to allow for the adjustment of vision when moving from one extreme of lighting to another, e.g. from outside to inside a building. Figure 2   – Control measures for pedestrian routes, walkways and work area zones Key: 1.   Pedestrian walkway zone coloured green 2.   Deflection zone added to the pedestrian zone marked with a white line 3.   Signage to mark and identify separate pedestrian and traffic routes 4.   Raised kerb to define traffic and pedestrian routes 5.   Marked crossing point with a green pull gate and offset doorway 6.   Entrance doorway
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.5.2 — Control measures for pedestrian routes, walkways and work area zones (p.16)

chunks.text: 9 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 4.5.2.2   A safety barrier that conforms to the recommendations in this PAS (see   Clause 5 ) should be used to segregate pedestrians and vehicles in the following circumstances: a)   when motorized vehicles are in operation; b)   when there is no raised kerb; c)   when the vehicle route is closer than 1 m to the pedestrian zone or walkway. NOTE 1   Markings such as white lines may be used for segregation in the following circumstances: a)   when manual and non-motorized vehicles are in operation; b)   when raised kerbs and vehicles routes are over 1m away from the pedestrian zone or walkway. NOTE 2   The 1 m area next to the pedestrian zone is called a pedestrian buffer zone. 4.5.2.3   Pedestrian barriers should be used at entrance points to prevent pedestrians walking in front of vehicles. 4.5.2.4   Doorway entrances should never open up to a pedestrian crossing point. NOTE 1   This stops pedestrians walking straight into a vehicle route without thinking and looking. NOTE 2   Green gates or markings help define exit points from pedestrian zones. 4.5.2.5   Gates should open towards the pedestrian to create a pause in pedestrian movement before leaving the pedestrian zone. 4.5.2.6   Pedestrian routes should be planned to correspond to the natural desire lines across the site. NOTE 1   This encourages pedestrians to stay on the designated safe routes and avoid potentially hazardous shortcuts. NOTE 2   Emergency exits may be designed to be directly opposite a pedestrian crossing point to enable a fast exit. 4.5.2.7   When desire lines cannot be used, a safety barrier should be used to stop pedestrians taking shortcuts and ensuring they stick to the walkway. NOTE   Strategically-placed mirrors can help pedestrians and vehicles see each other. Audible and/or visible warning devices, activated by movement, can help alert vehicle operators and pedestrians to each other’s presence. 4.6 Vehicle routes 4.6.1 Assessing a
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.6.1 — Assessing and evaluating (p.17)

chunks.text: vehicles see each other. Audible and/or visible warning devices, activated by movement, can help alert vehicle operators and pedestrians to each other’s presence. 4.6 Vehicle routes 4.6.1 Assessing and evaluating Assessment and evaluation of vehicle routes should be carried out by: a)   defining the pedestrian zones that are nearest to the traffic route; and b)   reviewing the suitability of the traffic route. 4.6.2 Control measures for vehicle routes Vehicle traffic routes should be, if possible: a)   free from pedestrians; b)   defined by safety barriers which are placed in position to guide vehicles; c)   a one-way system designed/organized to minimize the need for reversing and with adequate passing space around stationary vehicles; d)   wide enough for the largest vehicle that uses them; e)   free from sharp or blind bends, as reasonably practicable; f)   constructed from material able to safely bear loads that pass over them; g)   free from steep slopes; NOTE   If steep slopes cannot be avoided, the slopes are to be signposted. h)   well-maintained and free from obstructions; and i)   marked with correct health and safety signage. NOTE   Figure 3 shows an example of such traffic routes.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.6.1 — Assessing and evaluating (p.17)

chunks.text: 10 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 Figure 3   – Example of control measures for vehicle routes Key: 1.   Defined safety barriers 2.   Markings of a one-way system for vehicles 3.   Vehicle aisle width 4.   Signposted slope 4.7 Crossing points 4.7.1 Assessing and evaluating crossing points A crossing point is a critical area where a pedestrian and vehicle route cross; this should be assessed in the following ways: a)   How many crossing points are there and can the traffic routes be organized to reduce or eliminate crossing points? b)   Are crossing points properly managed to eliminate risks? c)   Are crossing points clearly visible? 4.7.2 Control measures for crossing points Traffic routes and work areas should be designed to eliminate or reduce crossing points to a minimum and then managed in the following ways: a)   use of overhead walkways, where possible; b)   crossing points made highly visible, using ground markings, lights and signs; c)   crossing points positioned where they are clearly visible for both pedestrians and vehicle drivers; d)   safety barriers positioned around the crossing point with pull gates; e)   entrance doorways not positioned directly opposite crossing points; and f)   crossing points designed so pedestrians pause and consider before moving through them. NOTE 1   Figure 4 shows an example of some of these control measures. NOTE 2   Traffic light systems may be used to manage the flow of vehicles and pedestrians. NOTE 3   A fire exit may be positioned opposite a crossing point.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.7.1 — Assessing and evaluating crossing points (p.18)

chunks.text: 11 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 Figure 4   – Example of control measures for crossing points Key: 1.   Overhead walkway 2.   Marked crossing point 3.   Pull gates at the crossing point to create a pause effect 4.   Entrance doorway where the crossing point is offset to create a pause effect 5.   Traffic light system to manage vehicle and pedestrian flow 4.8 Critical structures and equipment 4.8.1 Assessing and evaluating critical structures and equipment 4.8.1.1   Identification and location of critical structures and equipment within the workplace should be established. 4.8.1.2   The traffic and vehicle routes in conjunction with the critical structures and equipment should then be defined. 4.8.2 Control measures for critical structures and equipment Whenever possible, vehicles and vehicle routes should be moved away from any critical structure or equipment. If this is not possible critical structures and equipment should be protected in the following ways: a)   design the workplace layout to reduce the speed of vehicles around the area; NOTE   For example use speed bumps or other layouts and devices to reduce the vehicle speed. b)   where vehicles move near structures and equipment, use an appropriate safety barrier to protect them; c)   use highly visible items such as bollards, signs and ground markings to help guide drivers and identify critical points; d)   use safety barriers or road design to funnel drivers through entrances and doorways and direct vehicles away from critical points, such as door frames; e)   position and use safety barrier designs that help remove sharp corners; and f)   use safety barriers that are not in contact with structures or equipment and position them at distance that takes in consideration impact deflection distance. NOTE   Figure 5 shows an example of some of these control measures.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.8.1 — Assessing and evaluating critical structures and equipment (p.19)

chunks.text: 12 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 Figure 5   – Example of control measures for critical structures and equipment Key: 1.   Safety barrier protecting a column structure 2.   Safety barrier protecting the wall 3.   Safety barrier goal post protecting the doorway and surround 4.   Funnel designed safety barrier to guide vehicles through the doorway entrance/exit 4.9 Other areas where safety barriers aid and benefit traffic management 4.9.1 Vehicle parking areas The location and suitability of commercial and private parking areas should be evaluated to ensure the safest movement of pedestrians and vehicles. NOTE   Ideally, a pedestrian walkway leads to and from parking areas, using appropriate separation and safety barriers. 4.9.2 Loading and unloading zones When operating around loading and unloading zones: a)   wherever possible remove pedestrians from the area; b)   create a pedestrian safe zone next to the vehicle where the driver stays, if it is necessary for drivers to leave a vehicle; c)   segregate pedestrian zones with a safety barrier; d)   manage the situation so pedestrians and moving vehicles are not in the same area at the same time.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §4.9.1 — Vehicle parking areas (p.20)

chunks.text: 13 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 5 Safety barrier design 5.1   A safety barrier should be tested to a performance rating using the dynamic test method described in Clause 7 . The test should be performed by, or witnessed and supervised by, an independently-accredited test house or accreditation body. 5.2   The selected safety barrier should be manufactured using established documentation and within a maintained factory production quality control system. This factory production quality control system should consist of best practice procedures, regular inspections, routine tests and/or assessments and these results used to control raw or other incoming materials. NOTE   Attention is drawn to BS EN ISO 9001 or any equivalent quality control system. 5.3   The environmental temperature of the barrier should reflect the temperature of the test environment. 5.4   A safety barrier height should be at a level where the vehicle can be contained and not ride over the safety barrier. NOTE   An example is shown in Figure 6. Figure 6   – Example of correct and incorrect safety barrier heights 5.5   Ground level protection (as shown in Figure 7), should be provided in the following scenarios where there is risk of a fork or protruding item running against a safety barrier: a)   when a vehicle fork has a 20 m or greater travel run at a safety barrier; and b)   where critical building structures or equipment such as electrical equipment, sprinklers and columns are protected. 5.6   The ground level protection should have a minimum height of 150 mm.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §5.1 — Safety barrier performance rating (dynamic test) (p.21)

When the pedestrian zone or pedestrian walkway leads to an emergency exit or fire exit, the total space should be a minimum of 750 mm to 1 050 mm depending on the maximum number of people using the exit [4]. NOTE 1   The space for emergency exit access may include the deflection space of the safety barrier described in 5.10 . The width of escape routes is shown in Annex A, Table A.1. For example, 600 mm and 150 mm deflection means an escape route of 750 mm; if the deflection of the safety barrier is greater than 150 mm then the aisle width needs to be wider to suit. NOTE 2   Disabled access may be considered when necessary. A guide of 1 200 mm may be used for the space required. 5.10   The deflection of a safety barrier under maximum impact, carried out according to the test method described in   Clause 7 , should be added to the safety zone when defining the safe pedestrian area behind a safety barrier. The deflection zone should be marked out with a white line, as shown in Figure 9. NOTE   The space is defined by maximum deflection plus 600 mm.

Example of undamaged critical equipment 5.14   A risk assessment should be carried out to determine if people are likely to work in the area. If the risk assessment determines that people are likely to work in the area: a)   the safety barrier should have a handrail or balustrade when pedestrians are present and need to hold onto it; and b)   the handrails or balustrading should be 1 100 mm in height as, shown in Figure 12. NOTE 1   This height is recommended for the following reasons: a)   the barrier will not be easily climbable; and b)   it stops a person leaning over the barrier and it being a fulcrum to waist height. chunks.text: 20 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 5.18   The pull-out force of the fixture system on the substrate should be tested. The testing should conform to BS 5080. NOTE 1   The test is to ensure the substrate is strong enough to withstand impact loads that may transmit through the fixture system from the safety barrier during impact. NOTE 2   The manufacturer of the fixture system can state a maximum pull-out force for particular grades of concrete. The test can ascertain whether the fixture system meets this pull-out force, i.e. whether the quality of the concrete or substrate is adequate. NOTE 3   See the Construction Fixings Association website at www.fixingscfa.co.uk for more information [6]. 5.19   In the event that a safety barrier is damaged or it is known to have been impacted, it should be examined by a competent person to determine the condition of it and the fixture system. Damaged safety barriers or fixture systems should be completely replaced with new barriers and fixture systems. NOTE   Replacements may be a component instead of the whole system, for example replacement of a post, rail or fitting. chunks.text: 21 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 6 Kinetic energy of vehicles 6.1 How to calculate the kinetic energy of vehicles in a workplace In order to establish which safety barrier to use within a workplace environment, the kinetic energy of the vehicles operating should be calculated using the equation: KE = ½ m x (vsin Θ ) 2 where: •   KE is the kinetic energy (in joules); •   m is the mass of the vehicle (in kg); •   v is the velocity of the vehicle (in m/s); •   Θ   is the impact angle. NOTE   To simplify this calculation, the following figures for common angles may be used for the sin Θ   calculation: •   Sin90 ° = 1 •   Sin67.5 ° = 0.924 •   Sin45 ° = 0.707 •   Sin22.5 ° = 0.383 •   Sin10 ° = 0.1736 6.2 How to calculate the maximum vehicle impact angle in its direction of travel NOTE 1   The impact angles when a vehicle strikes a barrier affects the kinetic energy (KE) transferred, as shown in Figure 16. For example, if a vehicle impacts a barrier at an angle less than 90 °, the kinetic energy transferred to the barrier is less than when the vehicle impacts at 90 °. NOTE 2   Some workplace environments have vehicles moving parallel to safety barriers. This means the vehicles are very unlikely to impact the barriers head-on.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §6.1 — How to calculate the kinetic energy of vehicles in a workplace (p.29)

chunks.text: 26 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7 Methods of testing the force of impact that a safety barrier can withstand 7.1 Principle A test barrier is exposed to a controlled dynamic impact from a striker. The test barrier is impacted in a dynamic motion to measure its resistance. The test also measures the deflection of the test barrier and the loads seen by the fixture systems and ground. The process is: a)   Decide if you require to performance rate a safety barrier, ground level protection or both. The two tests are: 1)   performance rating a safety barrier; and 2)   performance rating ground level protection. b)   Select the appropriate striker for the chosen test or tests: 1)   a bumper striker is used to performance rate the safety barrier; 2)   a two-fork striker is used to performance rate ground level protection; c)   Select just one of three test apparatus listed below, to impact the appropriate striker onto the test barrier. 1)   a pendulum; 2)   a vehicle; or 3)   a sled and ramp. d)   Repeat the test at least once with a new test barrier to ensure the safety barrier performance rating and the results are consistent. NOTE 1   One test or both tests are executed depending if safety barrier is being used as a general safety barrier or/ and used for ground level protection. NOTE 2   See   5.5   for when ground level protection is required. 7.2 General apparatus NOTE   The following apparatus is used in each of the pendulum, vehicle or sled and ramp tests. 7.2.1   Test barriers and fixture system , for analysis and measurement. NOTE 1   A minimum of two barriers are used for each test performed. NOTE 2   The fixture system is used to anchor the test barrier to the ground conforming to the manufacturer’s instructions. 7.2.2   Line marking material , to mark out the test area and deflection area. 7.2.3   Load cell , with a display capable of taking a minimum of 100 samples per second. 7.2.4   High-speed camera , with a minimum frame rate of 200
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.2.1 — Test barriers and fixture system (p.34)

chunks.text: , to mark out the test area and deflection area. 7.2.3   Load cell , with a display capable of taking a minimum of 100 samples per second. 7.2.4   High-speed camera , with a minimum frame rate of 200 frames per second. 7.2.5   Standard camera , for visual inspection evidence. 7.2.6   Weights , added or removed from test apparatus depending on the mass required for the test as described in the test procedure. NOTE   As an example, the mass of the individual weights could be 25 kg each and the weights could take the form of steel plates or steel blocks.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.2.1 — Test barriers and fixture system (p.34)

chunks.text: 27 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.2.7   Weighing scales ,   to measure the weight of components. 7.2.8   Certified surveying equipment , to measure post-spacing, rail heights, alignment orientation and any critical items. 7.2.9   Safety net , with a 50 mm mesh made from a high-strength nylon to stop any debris flying out during the test. 7.2.10   Bumper striker , a segment of circle of radius 725 mm with a chord of 1 000 mm and a height of 200 mm used with the test to rate the performance of a safety barrier, integrated with the test equipment used on the test and constructed of materials that will not deform or absorb energy during an impact and withstands repeated impact events. NOTE   The bumper striker can be used with the pendulum, vehicle or sled and ramp test equipment. An example of a bumper striker is shown in Figure 19. Figure 19   – Example of a bumper striker 7.2.11   Two-fork striker , forks constructed of materials that do not deform or absorb energy during an impact and withstands repeated impact events; fork tips are vertical flat face, 50 mm wide and 10 mm thickness with a surface area of 500 mm 2 ; the distance between the centre of one fork to the second is 700 mm. NOTE   The two-fork striker is used to performance rate ground level protection. It can be used with pendulum, vehicle or sled and ramp test equipment. An example of a two-fork striker is shown in Figure 20.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.2.10 — Bumper striker (p.35)

chunks.text: 28 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 Figure 20   – Example of a two-fork striker dimensions Key: 1.   Distance between the forks 2.   Fork width 3.   Fork length 7.3 General preparation 7.3.1   A safety zone is set out for any observers and the safety net is put around the front and sides of the test barrier to capture any flying debris. 7.3.2   A minimum of two test barriers are used for each test performed. 7.3.3   Each test barrier is positioned in the test area and acclimatized to the temperature and humidity level required for the testing, for at least four hours. 7.3.4   Specifications of each test barrier, including the engineering drawings, are documented. NOTE   See example shown in Figure 21.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.3 — General preparation (p.36)

chunks.text: 29 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 Figure 21   – Example of a safety barrier drawing and specification sheet Key: 1.   Horizontal rail 2.   Vertical post 3.   Floor plate 7.3.5   Each engineering drawing identifies the test barrier in exact detail, showing arrangement, locations and dimensions of all components. 7.3.6   Specifications for materials used are included in all documents. 7.3.7   Any structures used to support the barrier structure and resist induced forces during the test impacts are specified and documented. 7.3.8   The test barrier is attached to solid ground or a frame using a fixture system that is installed to the manufacturer’s instructions. 7.3.9   Lines on the floor are marked at 50 mm intervals behind the test barrier to enable measurement of deflection. The measurement is then taken using the high-speed camera software and film recording. 7.3.10   The high-speed camera is positioned in line with the test barrier in order to record the impact. The marked lines are in view of the high speed camera. 7.3.11   A load cell is positioned on the fixture system of the test barrier to measure the transmitting force. 7.3.12   The test barrier is aligned so that the striker impacts it at its central point and 90 ° to the striker.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.3 — General preparation (p.37)

chunks.text: 30 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.3.13   The test area and test barrier are photographed before and after each test is performed. NOTE   An example of the test barrier is shown in Figure 22. This figure shows a pendulum being used to test the barrier but the test barrier set-up is the same if a vehicle or sled and ramp are used. Figure 22   – Example of a test barrier (using pendulum equipment) with lines marked on the ground to measure deflection Key: 1.   High speed camera 2.   Test barrier 3.   Load cell located on the fixture system 4.   Pendulum frame 5.   Bumper striker 6.   Pendulum arm 7.   Marked lines on the floor 7.4 General procedure 7.4.1   The bumper striker is used for the safety barrier performance test and the two-fork striker is used for the ground level protection performance test. 7.4.2   The test barrier is subjected to a defined amount of kinetic energy. If the test is performed on a test barrier that has rails, the test is performed on the following component targets of the barrier: 1)   the mid-post, if present and structurally different from the end post, at a height level with the centre of the rail; 2)   the end post at a height level with the centre of the rail; and 3)   on the rail at the point central between two posts and central in height of the rail.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.4 — General procedure (p.38)

chunks.text: 31 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 If the test barrier has no rails, the test is performed on the central part of the test barrier at a height of between 200-500 mm. NOTE 1   Figure 23 shows an example of these test targets. NOTE 2   It is important that the testing impact height and safety barrier design match the impact heights of the vehicles with which they are intended to be used (see   5.4 ). NOTE 3   The test is the same for both a general safety barrier performance rating and ground level protection performance rating. Figure 23   – Examples of where to impact safety barrier components Key: 1.   End-post test 2.   Centre of rail test 3.   Mid-post test 4.   Central part of stand-alone test barrier 7.4.3   The tests on the components are performed at least two times with new test barriers for each test, to evaluate the sample consistency. 7.4.4   Record the environmental temperature and test the barrier at the highest and lowest temperatures in which it will be used. 7.4.5   Quarantine the test area to keep pedestrians a safe distance from the impact zone. 7.4.6   Use a safety factor of 5% in the tests. The test barrier is rated at 5% less than the kinetic energy impacted on it. 7.4.7   Turn on the high-speed camera before the striker is released to capture and record the impact for analysis. 7.4.8   Record the results of the impact test in the test report. NOTE 1   The impact ultimately determines if the test barrier, test barrier components and the fixture systems used can withstand the transferred kinetic energy. NOTE 2   Annex C shows an example of a test report that may be used.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.4 — General procedure (p.39)

chunks.text: 32 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.5 Pendulum test 7.5.1 Pendulum apparatus to impact the strikers onto the barrier Pendulum , constructed of rigid material that allows maximum kinetic energy transfer to the test barrier; pendulum arm is free to swing with minimum resistance to movement; the mechanism for releasing the pendulum from its initial position on the frame operates freely and permits release of the pendulum without initial impulse, retardation or side vibration. NOTE   An example of the pendulum is shown in Figure 24. Figure 24   – Example of a pendulum using a bumper striker Key: 1.   Bumper striker 2.   Weights 3.   Pendulum frame 7.5.2 Pendulum impact test preparation 7.5.2.1   Select the bumper striker or two-fork striker depending on the test type chosen. 7.5.2.2   Secure or bolt the striker and make it integral to the pendulum arm. 7.5.2.3   The test barrier is positioned so the pendulum arm will strike it horizontal at the point of impact (i.e. at 90 ° to the barrier), thereby transferring the maximum kinetic energy at the point of impact. 7.5.2.4   Weights can be added or removed from the pendulum and these are weighed on a commercial certified scale in order to determine the mass. 7.5.2.5   The weights are bolted and made integral to the pendulum. NOTE 1   Figure 25 shows an example of a bumper striker horizontal (at 90 °) at impact against the test barrier. NOTE 2   The barrier position might have to change in order to keep the striker impact position horizontal according to the type of striker; for example if the fork striker is longer than the bumper striker and this means the barrier position moving further away.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.5.1 — Pendulum apparatus to impact the strikers onto the barrier (p.40)

Example of a fork striker 7.5.2.6   Each time a different striker is used, the mass of the striker is independently weighed and recorded using scales. 7.5.2.7   The velocity of the pendulum arm is calculated and certified. The velocity may be approximated from the gravity equation for a weight dropping from a height. The height is determined by the angle from the vertical that is the starting point of the pendulum’s swing. Thus, the velocity at the bottom of the swing can be determined using the following equation: v      2      g      L      ( 1      cos(a) ) where: •   v is the velocity of the weight at the striker position (in m/s); •   g is the acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s 2 ); •   L is the length of the pendulum arm (in m); •   (a) is the angle from the vertical.

Fork lift truck 7.6.2 Vehicle impact test preparation 7.6.2.1   The vehicle is weighed on a commercial certified scale in order to determine its mass. 7.6.2.2   The vehicle bumper or two-fork striker will face the test barrier depending on the test type chosen. 7.6.2.3   The strikers are integral to the vehicle. 7.6.2.4   The test barrier is positioned and lined-up so the vehicle is in a straight line and will impact at the desired location on the test barrier. The vehicle is accelerated in a straight line. 7.6.2.5   Weights can be added or removed from the vehicle and these are weighed on a commercial certified scale in order to determine the mass. 7.6.2.6   The weights are bolted and made integral to the vehicle. 7.6.2.7   The equipment to measure velocity is set up; for example, a speed trap consisting of two mats or lasers is set up to capture the speed before impact. NOTE   Figure 28 shows an example of vehicle impact test preparation area.

High speed camera 3.   Speed trap 4.   Marked lines to measure deflection during impact 5.   Test barrier 6.   Vehicle 7.   Example of a bumper striker 8.   Example of a fork striker 7.6.3 Vehicle impact test procedure 7.6.3.1   The speed of the vehicle or sled during the test is 5 to 15 km/h. NOTE   This speed is to provide a realistic performance rating of the safety barrier. 7.6.3.2   Determine the vehicle   mass and/or the vehicle impact speed required so as to measure and classify the performance of the barrier including the 5% safety factor using the equations in   7.6.3.2   and   7.6.3.3 . 7.6.3.3   To determine the mass of the vehicle or sled required: m      2      E      105% v 2 where: •   m is the mass of the vehicle (kg); •   E is the impact energy required in the test (J); •   v is the velocity of the vehicle at impact (m/s). EXAMPLE: Example for a vehicle or sled requiring a 15 000 J impact energy impacting at 4.47 m/s: m      2      15 000      105%      1 576 kg 4.47   2
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.6.3 — Vehicle impact test procedure (p.45)

chunks.text: 38 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.6.3.4   To determine the velocity of vehicle or sled required: v      2      E      105% m EXAMPLE: Example for a vehicle or sled requiring a 15 000 J impact energy and a mass of 1 576 kg: v      2      15 000      105%      4.47 m/s 1 576 7.6.3.5   Line-up the vehicle at a distance from the test barrier to allow for the desired velocity. 7.6.3.6   Using an acceleration method, accelerate the vehicle towards the test barrier target area. NOTE   The vehicle may be accelerated into the barrier using: a)   a winch to pull the vehicle that will disengage before impact on the test barrier; b)   something to push the vehicle, i.e. a person, a workplace vehicle or ram; or c)   a natural downward gradient to use gravitational energy. 7.6.3.7   The vehicle striker impacts against the test barrier at an angle of ±2 ° on the specified target point. NOTE   Figure 29 shows an example of a vehicle impacting a test barrier. Figure 29   – Example of a vehicle impacting a test barrier Key: 1.   3D image of a vehicle test procedure 2.   High speed camera 3.   Speed trap 4.   Marked lines to measure deflection during impact 5.   Test barrier 6.   Vehicle
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.6.3 — Vehicle impact test procedure (p.46)

chunks.text: 39 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.6.3.8   Each time a different striker is used, the mass of the striker is independently weighed and recorded using scales. 7.6.3.9   Record the results of the impact tests in the test report. 7.7 Sled and ramp impact test 7.7.1 Apparatus 7.7.1.1   Sled , constructed of rigid material that allows maximum kinetic energy transfer to the test barrier, with resistance-free wheels that fit onto the ramp tracks. NOTE   The sled wheels are designed to support the sled’s maximum applicable weight and not suffer any obstruction during the roll downwards the inclined plane, so that it replicates the moving industrial vehicle. 7.7.1.2   Ramp , a solid structure with an inclined plane at a pre-defined angle, with parallel tracks or rails integrated onto the ramp to guide the sled. 7.7.1.3   Winch , to pull the sled up to the desired height. 7.7.1.4   Speed trap , used to measure velocity, may be constructed of two mats or of two lasers. NOTE   An example of sled and ramp is shown in Figure 30. Figure 30   – Example of a sled and ramp Key: 1.   Ramp with guide tracks and winch 2.   Forks striker 3.   Sled 4.   Weights 7.7.2 Sled and ramp impact test preparation 7.7.2.1   The sled is weighed on a commercial certified scale in order to determine its mass. 7.7.2.2   Select the bumper striker or two-fork striker depending on the test type chosen. 7.7.2.3   Secure or bolt the striker and make it integral to the sled.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.7.1 — Apparatus (p.47)

chunks.text: 40 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.7.2.4   Weights can be added or removed from the sled and these are weighed on a commercial certified scale in order to determine the mass. The weights are bolted and made integral to the sled. 7.7.2.5   The test barrier is positioned and lined-up so that the sled striker will impact at the desired location on the test barrier. NOTE   Figure 31 shows an example of sled and ramp impact test preparation area. Figure 31   – Example of a ramp and sled impact test preparation area Key: 1.   Sled with bumper striker 2.   Sled with fork striker 3.   Winch 4.   Weights bolted and integral to the sled 5.   Sled going down the ramp 6.   Rails or track to guide the sled 7.   Ramp 8.   Speed trap 9.   High speed camera 10.   Test barrier 11.   Marked lines to measure deflection during impact 7.7.2.6   The equipment to measure the velocity is set up; for example, a speed trap consisting of two mats or lasers is set up to capture the speed before impact. 7.7.3 Sled and ramp impact test procedure 7.7.3.1   Determine the sled   mass and/or the sled impact speed required so as to measure and classify the performance of the barrier including the 5% safety factor. NOTE   The calculation to determine the sled mass or the sled speed is the same as described in 7.6.3.1 for the vehicle impact test procedure.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.7.3 — Sled and ramp impact test procedure (p.48)

chunks.text: 41 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.7.3.2   The sled is pulled up the ramp by a winch to a height to allow for the desired velocity. 7.7.3.3   Disengage the winch allowing the sled to accelerate down the ramp towards the test barrier. 7.7.3.4   The sled striker impacts against the test barrier at an angle of ±2 ° on the specified target point. NOTE   Figure 32 shows an example of a sled impacting a test barrier. Figure 32   – Example of a sled impacting a test barrier Key: 1.   Winch 2.   Tracks on the ramp to guide the sled 3.   Weights on the sled 4.   Speed trap 5.   High speed camera 6.   Impacted test barrier 7.7.3.5   Each time a different striker is used, the mass of the striker is independently weighed and recorded using scales. 7.7.3.6   Record the results of the impact tests in the test report. 7.8 Pass and fail criteria 7.8.1   Pass criteria for the test barrier: a)   the test barrier has arrested the striker; b)   the vehicle, sled or striker has not breached the test barrier; c)   there is no catastrophic failure in the test barrier after the test; d)   the fixture system is not damaged, remains in the substrate and has a lower tested pull-out force than the manufactured rated pull-out force.
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.8 — Pass and fail criteria (p.49)

chunks.text: 42 PAS 13: 2017 © The British Standards Institution 2017 7.8.2   Fail criteria for the test barrier: a)   the test barrier does not arrest the striker, vehicle or sled; b)   the vehicle, sled or striker breaches the test barrier; c)   there is catastrophic failure within the test barrier; NOTE   Catastrophic failure is when any component that is structurally integral to the test barrier is sheared or damaged beyond performance, even if the striker, vehicle or sled does not breach the barrier. Catastrophic failure is judged from a visual inspection and high speed camera recordings by the person compiling the test report. Sacrificial parts and cosmetic items are excluded. d)   fixings fail because the tested measured force upon the fixings is higher than the manufacturer’s rated pull-out force. The fixings also fail if they pull out of the floor or substrate. 7.9 Expression of results 7.9.1   The test data collected includes: a)   testing facility and environmental data; b)   test method and apparatus; c)   barrier and fixing system; and d)   results, including pass or fail, deflection, minor damage and description. 7.9.2   The data can be collated onto a test report (see Annex C for an example test report).
chunks.label: PAS 13 §7.9 — Expression of results (p.50)

chunks.text: Health and safety is a line all businesses must draw. The line between vehicles and pedestrians. The line that creates a safe working environment for everyone. Workplaces are fraught with dangers. And collisions with vehicles can be fatal. Workplace safety is a global problem. PAS 13 is the global solution. With a steering committee made up of the world's biggest companies from every industry and sector. An initiative sponsored by us. With technical leadership by world-leading experts at our headquarters. A code of practice revolutionising workplace safety. So whether you're a factory manager, health and safety manager, or anyone caring for staff and facilities, adopt PAS 13 and join a global effort to draw a line under workplace safety.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13: the safety barrier code of practice improving industrial safety standards worldwide
chunks.label: PAS 13: the safety barrier code of practice improving industrial safety standards worldwide @ 0:00

chunks.text: To be 100% effective, barrier heights must match the impact zone of vehicle types in operation at a workplace. Consider the height of the impact zone of vehicles and ensure barriers have been tested to dynamic real-life simulation at speed. Vehicle fork protection at ground level is essential in environments where vehicle forks could pass beneath the barriers. Key infrastructure such as door frames, columns, sprinkler systems and electrical equipment, as well as valuable machinery, should also have protection at an appropriate height, with minimum ground level height of 150mm. Ground level protection must be used to prevent serious injury.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS13 code of practice explains safety barrier height, impact zone and fork protection
chunks.label: PAS13 code of practice explains safety barrier height, impact zone and fork protection @ 0:01

chunks.text: In a busy workplace environment, basic road safety steps are often dangerously ignored. Crossing points are accident hotspots. Minimise their use through effective segregation and traffic flow management. Where crossing is unavoidable, an overhead walkway is the safest solution. If an overhead solution is not possible, effective crossing points are essential. They can prompt both pedestrians and drivers to stop, look, listen. Where pedestrians cross frequently, a safe cross-gate configuration may be the safest solution. Consider every element of the crossing point, from both a vehicle and pedestrian perspective, such as offset doorways to slow pedestrians and floor markings as visual alerts for drivers. Pull gates force pedestrians to break stride, creating a pause effect and prompting a moment to think and look.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial facility crossing points
chunks.label: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial facility crossing points @ 0:01

chunks.text: But ensure they open towards emergency exits so as not to hinder evacuation.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial facility crossing points
chunks.label: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial facility crossing points @ 1:07

chunks.text: Correct use of handrails and balustrades can be life-saving. With a low fulcrum point, the barrier function is inadequate. Falling over the side could be fatal. With a high fulcrum point, a person cannot fall over the barrier. Barriers must be able to support two adults leaning. They should avoid sharp edges and have smooth surface areas to reduce risk of injury.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS13 code of practice tips on safety barrier handrails for pedestrian walkways
chunks.label: PAS13 code of practice tips on safety barrier handrails for pedestrian walkways @ 0:01

chunks.text: Vehicle routes should be free of pedestrians, work within a one-way system and be wide enough for the vehicles using it. Vehicle routes and pedestrian routes are most effective when planned together. Avoid slopes, use a funnel system to direct vehicles away from critical infrastructure and avoid sharp blind bends with appropriate protection and clear signage. Routes should be clear of obstructions and surfaces maintained. For more information, visit www.fema.gov
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial traffic management and vehicle routes
chunks.label: PAS 13 code of practice tips on industrial traffic management and vehicle routes @ 0:00

chunks.text: CONSIDERING LINE OF VISION Considering line of vision is essential when planning workplace protection. Pay attention to both pedestrian and vehicle points of view. The line of vision for forklift truck drivers is different to that of pedestrians. DRIVERS OF LARGE, ARTICULATED VEHICLES Drivers of large, articulated vehicles have an elevated line of vision, which must be accounted for. When employed properly, the visual alert function of barriers and bollards can be enough to avoid impacts. CONSIDERING LINE OF VISION
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Accounting for Line of Vision
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Accounting for Line of Vision @ 0:01

chunks.text: A PAS 13 compliant safety barrier must segregate pedestrians when motorised vehicles are in operation, if the vehicle route is closer than one metre and there is no raised kerb. Where only light, hand-operated machinery, such as pallet trucks, are working alongside pedestrians, safety barriers are not required. DESIRE LINES The paths created by the human instinct to take the easiest route. In the workplace, this instinct can be dangerous. Design pedestrian routes to correspond to DESIRE LINES if possible. Where not possible, guide employees to follow a safer path. WALKWAYS Walkways must be a suitable width for two people walking side by side, with increased width for wheelchair access.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Effective Pedestrian Segregation
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Effective Pedestrian Segregation @ 0:01

chunks.text: DEFLECTION Deflection is the distance a barrier bends under impact. The deflection zone must be wide enough to allow for this, should impact occur. A deflection zone helps to ensure pedestrian safety at all times. RAISED KERBS Raised kerbs prevent vehicles from straying from designated routes. Consider vision adjustment and ensure pedestrians enter a safe zone when crossing between different light levels. MARK ROUTES WITH CLEAR SIGNAGE AND APPROPRIATE COLOUR DEMARCATION BARRIER GATES SHOULD BE OFFSET FROM DOORWAYS AND OPEN INWARDS, CAUSING PEDESTRIANS TO BREAK STRIDE, CREATING A PAUSE EFFECT TO PROMPT A MOMENT TO THINK AND LOOK.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Effective Pedestrian Segregation
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Effective Pedestrian Segregation @ 1:03

chunks.text: In a busy facility, valuable equipment and structures are at risk of damage. Assets such as electrical equipment, columns and door frames. Structures must be protected from vehicle forks at ground level. Key machinery and structures must be identified and protected. Use highly visible bollards and barriers for protection. Signposts and floor markings to create visual alerts for drivers. Funnel-designed entranceways direct vehicles away from critical infrastructure, like door frames. Soften sharp corners to remove vulnerable impact spots. All barriers deflect, so cannot function properly if in contact with or close to critical structures and equipment. Without an appropriate deflection zone, impacts can force the barrier into the machinery it protects.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 code of practice industrial safety tips for protecting buildings and assets
chunks.label: PAS 13 code of practice industrial safety tips for protecting buildings and assets @ 0:01

chunks.text: Visibility saves lives. A barrier's first function is as a visual alert. Barriers must maintain high visibility through either continuous repainting or with self-coloured solutions. Yellow and black signifies caution. This high contrast colour pairing catches the eye and is a globally recognised hazard indicator. Red and white is a warning, danger. Green marks pedestrian safe zones and exit points. Proper application of these colours provides an instant visual alert for every employee.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Utilising Standard Colour Demarcation
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Utilising Standard Colour Demarcation @ 0:01

chunks.text: PAS compliant test method requires the following equipment. A minimum of two test barriers for each test performed and a fixture system to anchor the barrier to the ground with line marking material to mark out the test area and the deflection area. A load cell with a display capable of taking a minimum of 100 samples per second. A high speed camera with a minimum frame rate of 200 frames per second and a standard camera for visual inspection evidence. Weights should be added or removed from the test apparatus depending on the mass required for the test. Certified surveying equipment must be used to measure post spacing, rail heights, alignment orientation and any critical items. A bumper striker with the correct dimensions to replicate a forklift truck constructed of materials that will not deform or absorb energy during impact and withstand repeated impacts. A two fork striker is used to performance rate ground level protection.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Know Your Equipment
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Know Your Equipment @ 0:01

chunks.text: It too must be constructed of materials that do not deform and absorb energy during impact and can withstand repeated impacts. The forks must replicate the average forklift truck in an industrial environment. Each test barrier must be positioned in the test area and acclimatised to the temperature and humidity levels required for testing for at least four hours.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Know Your Equipment
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Know Your Equipment @ 1:05

chunks.text: Setting up the vehicle area requires a sled constructed of a rigid material that allows maximum kinetic energy to transfer into the test barrier with resistance-free wheels that can fit onto the ramp tracks. Sled wheels are designed to support the maximum applicable weight and not suffer any obstruction during the roll downwards to replicate the moving industrial vehicle. The ramp must also be a solid structure with an inclined plane at a predefined angle, with parallel tracks or rails integrated onto the ramp to guide the sled. A winch to pull the sled up to the required height to generate the energy desired for a particular test. A speed trap constructed of two mats or two lasers is used to measure the velocity of the vehicle when it impacts the barrier. The sled must be weighed on a commercially certified scale in order to determine its mass. The bumper striker or two-fork striker chosen for the test must be secured to the sled in a manner that makes it integral.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Makes a Compliant Impact Vehicle?
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Makes a Compliant Impact Vehicle? @ 0:01

chunks.text: Any weights added or removed from the sled to adjust its mass must be weighed on a commercially certified scale and bolted on. The test barrier must be positioned so that the sled striker will impact at the desired location on the barrier.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Makes a Compliant Impact Vehicle?
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Makes a Compliant Impact Vehicle? @ 1:05

chunks.text: Setting up the impact area requires the following steps. Prepare the bumper striker or forklift striker. Attach the sled to the winch and fit with weights to generate the desired impact energy. The sled must travel down the ramp guided by rails or a track to prevent scraping on the floor or diversion from the intended path. A speed trap verifies the velocity the sled is travelling. A high-speed camera captures details about the test for later analysis. The test barrier itself must be securely fixed in position with marked lines to measure deflection during impact.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: How Should an Impact Area be Prepared?
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: How Should an Impact Area be Prepared? @ 0:01

chunks.text: Once the equipment has been set up in both the vehicle and impact areas, the following procedure applies. The full test procedure must be applied to the following component targets of the barrier. The end post, the rail centre and the mid post. When testing a bollard, the test must be performed at a height of between 200 and 500 mm. Tests on each component target and the bollard must be performed at least twice. With new barriers for each new test, in order to evaluate the sample consistency. Record the environmental temperature and test the barrier at both the highest and lowest temperatures in which it will be used. Quarantine the test area to keep pedestrians a safe distance from the impact zone.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: The Steps to Follow
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: The Steps to Follow @ 0:01

chunks.text: Barrier suppliers must present an impact rating using a safety factor of 5%. This means the barrier is rated 5% lower than the kinetic energy impacted upon it in testing. Turn on the high-speed camera before the striker is released to capture and record the impact for analysis. Record the results of the impact in the test report.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: The Steps to Follow
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: The Steps to Follow @ 1:06

chunks.text: If the equipment set up for both the vehicle area and impact area and the procedural steps have been followed correctly, a full test run-through should look like this. For more information, visit www.fema.gov
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Does a Full Test Look Like?
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: What Does a Full Test Look Like? @ 0:00

chunks.text: The data required to be collected when testing a safety barrier must be compiled into a standardised report. This report details information about the test, such as the test method, a description of the barrier, the environment temperature and humidity, as well as the mass and the striker used and the kinetic energy being applied. The data includes the test barrier's deflection, force on the fixings, damage and whether it passes or fails the test. The full test procedure must be applied to the following component targets of the barrier. The end post, the rail centre and the mid post. You can find an example of a test barrier report in Annex C, page 45 of the PAS 13 document.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Recording and Reporting your Results
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Recording and Reporting your Results @ 0:01

chunks.text: There are four criteria by which a barrier can pass or fail the PAS testing procedure. Firstly, if the test barrier does not stop the striker, vehicle or sled. Secondly, if the striker, sled or vehicle breaches the test barrier. Thirdly, if there is catastrophic failure within the barrier by which it is rendered structurally unsound and damaged beyond performance. Finally, if the fixings fail by being pulled out of the floor or substrate. This might happen because the tested force upon them is higher than the manufacturer's rated pull-out force.
chunks.videoTitle: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Pass or Fail
chunks.label: PAS 13 Code of Practice | Barrier Testing: Pass or Fail @ 0:01

chunks.text:
Product: eFlex Pedestrian Barrier 3 Rail
Category: Pedestrian Barriers
Environment: internal, external
ATEX (explosive atmospheres): Not suitable — Not suitable in an explosive atmosphere
Standard base & fixing: bolt: 12mm Hex Head; fixing: Through Bolt, Z/P; base material: Zinc nickel, electrophoretic coating
Suitable vehicles/users: Pedestrians
Fit-for-purpose applications: Pedestrian segregation
Impact zone: 233 - 1100mm (9⅛ - 43⅓ inches)
Material properties: temp -10°C to 50°C; ignition 370°C to 390°C; flash point 350°C to 370°C; toxicity Not Hazardous; chemical resistance Excellent - ISO/TR 10358; weathering 5/5*; light stability 7/8**; static 10¹⁵ - 10¹⁶ Ω; hygiene seals: no

Suitable vehicles/users: Lightweight Counterbalance FLT; Electric Reach Truck; Electric Pedestrian Truck; Manual Pallet Truck; Electric Pedestrian Stacker
Fit-for-purpose applications: Building and equipment protection; Corridor and wall protection; Column protection; Projects machinery; Racking and storage protection
Impact zone: 205 - 395mm (8 - 15⅝ inches)
Material properties: temp -10°C to 50°C; ignition 370°C to 390°C; flash point 350°C to 370°C; toxicity Not Hazardous; chemical resistance Excellent - ISO/TR 10358; weathering 5/5*; light stability 7/8**; static 10¹⁵ - 10¹⁶ Ω; hygiene seals: yes
Fire information: Manufactured from polypropylene. Polypropylene has an ignition temperature of 370° Celsius which is significantly higher than materials such as wooden pallets or polymers such as flexible PVC. This means that for combustion of an A-SAFE barrier to occur, a facility fire would need to be both large and well advanced, by which time any personnel would have already been evacuated to safety.

Polymer has been engineered to remain stable up to a temperature of 350° Celsius. In contrast, PVC starts breaking down at between 249° Celsius and 273° Celsius, and materials such as wood will burn instantly.

Our material has been specially developed to minimise the release of smoke during combustion. Furthermore, unlike environmentally hazardous polymers like PVC, it does not release harmful dioxins, hydrogen cyanide or hydrogen chloride when burnt.
Environmental information: Research conducted by a leading plastic solutions company* showed that producing one metric tonne of polymer generates less carbon emissions than producing a tonne of steel**. Therefore, from the very outset of raw material production, polymer provides a significant CO₂ saving over steel.

For more information visit A-SAFE.com

Maintenance scope: GLOBAL
Applies to families: *
Inspection frequency: annual
Inspection notes: Base plate fixings: check torque setting every 12 months, or sooner if an impact has occurred. Pins & caps: check regularly for loosening from general use and cleaning — remove pin covers and retighten with the A-SAFE tool.
Cleaning: Regular cleaning reduces dirt and debris infiltrating crucial mechanical parts; lack of cleaning may shorten product lifespan and cause component failures. Carry out a full risk assessment before cleaning; wear gloves and eye protection; dispose of waste water per national regulations.
Damage assessment:
  • Check base plate fixing torque every 12 months — or immediately after any known impact.
  • Remove pin covers periodically and retighten pins with the A-SAFE tool if they have loosened.
Warranty conditions: A-SAFE disclaims all liability for injuries or ill health sustained during cleaning, maintenance or the use of chemical substances. Users are responsible for following proper safety procedures and using appropriate protective measures. Consult Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and Technical Data Sheets (TDS) from chemical suppliers before using any cleaning product on A-SAFE polymer. Product integrity warranties assume the inspection and cleaning cadence described in this document has been followed; damaged components (crease marks, cracks, base deformation, inner-layer deformation) must be replaced rather than repaired to preserve system integrity.
Recommended spares: Pin caps; Pins (rail + post); Base plate fixings
chunks.label: Product Maintenance (PRH-1001) — GLOBAL

chunks.text:
Maintenance scope: Memaplex Barrier Family
Applies to families: iFlex, FlexiShield, ShieldRail
Applies to categories: barrier, guardrail
Inspection frequency: annual
Inspection notes: Base plate fixings: check torque setting every 12 months, or sooner if an impact has occurred. Pins & caps: check regularly for loosening from general use and cleaning — remove pin covers and retighten with the A-SAFE tool.
Cleaning: Internal: warm water + kitchen cleaner on a non-abrasive cloth; wipe each rail and post, remove all dust/dirt from fixing points, then wipe dry. External: use a jet wash to lift stubborn dirt, then a stronger rubber-suited detergent on marked areas (see A-SAFE chemical-resistance list, pages 7–9 of PRH-1001). Never leave water pooled in fixing points. Full risk assessment before cleaning; gloves and eye protection throughout; dispose of waste water per national regulations.
Damage assessment:
  • Rail alignment: rail and coupling cover out of alignment — indicative of a reasonable-energy impact or repeated low-level impacts. If any further indicators are present, replace the affected section; otherwise inspect coupling, sleeve, rail pin, post pin and post in depth.
  • Light areas (post/rail whitening): material has been taken beyond its elastic limit. At least one reasonable-energy impact, or many lower-energy ones. Continuous inspection of the area recommended.
  • Crease markings: fatigue from repeated reasonable-energy impacts. Replace any section showing this — overall integrity is compromised.
  • Rail area lightening (between rail pin and post): repeated medium-energy impacts. Full guardrail inspection; replace affected parts.
  • Inner layer deformation: inner rail material visible around the coupling sleeve on the exterior — near-maximum-energy impact. Replace the affected system.
  • Base deformation: base deformed / fixings pulling out of the ground — near-maximum-energy impact. Replace the affected system.
  • Cracking: cracks anywhere in the system — severe damage events, integrity negated. Replace the affected parts.
  • Scratches and scrapes: deep scratches from light collisions with vehicles or pallets. Monitor quantity and outer shape — if any crack appears, replace the product.
Warranty conditions: A-SAFE disclaims all liability for injuries or ill health sustained during cleaning, maintenance or the use of chemical substances. Users are responsible for following proper safety procedures and using appropriate protective measures. Consult Safety Data Sheets (SDS) and Technical Data Sheets (TDS) from chemical suppliers before using any cleaning product on A-SAFE polymer. Product integrity warranties assume the inspection and cleaning cadence described in this document has been followed; damaged components (crease marks, cracks, base deformation, inner-layer deformation) must be replaced rather than repaired to preserve system integrity.
Recommended spares: Rail pins; Post pins; Pin caps; Couplings / coupling sleeves; Base plate fixings (to factory torque spec); Replacement rail sections (for creased / cracked rails); Replacement post sections (for cracked / lightened posts)
chunks.label: Product Maintenance (PRH-1001) — Memaplex Barrier Family

  • Cracking & splitting: split in the material from high-energy, high-speed impact. General inspection of rack leg; RackGuard must be removed and replaced.
